abstract: Comprehensive metabolome coverage remains a significant challenge in ‘omic sciences due to the vast chemical diversity and range of physical properties exhibited by small molecules. Standard untargeted workflows often suffer from polarity bias, where reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) favors lipophilic species and hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC) captures water-soluble compounds. In this study, we developed an integrated, dual-separation LC/MS approach to bridge this gap. We optimized an aminopropyl-based HILIC/MS method, identifying that a basic mobile phase (pH 9.8) with specific ammonium additives provided superior resolution and sensitivity for polar analytes. This method demonstrated high chromatographic reproducibility and robustness over extended run times, facilitating the accurate assessment of dysregulated metabolites in central carbon pathways—such as organic acids and phosphorylated sugars—that are typically lost in the RPLC void volume. Applying a single-extraction, dual-separation workflow (RPLC and HILIC in both ESI positive and negative modes) to bacterial extracts, human plasma, and cancer cells enabled the detection of over 30,000 features per sample type. Our results indicate that the most extensive biological information, spanning both lipid and central carbon metabolism, is achieved by combining RPLC ESI(+) and HILIC ESI(-) modes. This integrative strategy provides a reliable framework for expanding metabolome coverage toward the ‘omic scale.
